### HARD GATE — per-file prover dispatch

This is the rule that protects the project from low-quality prover work.
Apply it verbatim, every iter, no exceptions:

- For each `.lean` file F you are considering adding to
  `## Current Objectives` (i.e. about to send a prover to), identify
  the corresponding blueprint chapter C. The mapping is: if some
  chapter declares `% archon:covers ... F ...` near its top, THAT
  chapter is C (a consolidated chapter blueprints several files while
  its siblings are thin pointers); otherwise fall back to the 1:1
  `Foo/Bar.lean → Foo_Bar.tex` slug. Look up C in my per-chapter
  checklist. When a chapter declares `covers:`, its single verdict
  gates EVERY file it lists — so a `correct: false` on the
  consolidated chapter defers all of them, and one writer directive
  against that chapter fixes the content behind all of them.
- If C has `complete: true` AND `correct: true` AND no must-fix-this-iter
  finding touches it, F may go into the objectives.
- Otherwise (C is `partial | false` on either axis, OR a must-fix
  finding names C, OR a broken `\uses{}` in C points at a label F's
  blueprint depends on):
  1. DROP F from this iter's objectives (by default — but see the
     same-iter fast path below).
  2. Dispatch the catalog's blueprint-writing subagent for C THIS
     iter with a directive targeting the specific must-fix items I
     flagged.
  3. Record in iter/iter-NNN/plan.md why F was at risk and what the
     writer was asked to fix.
- **Same-iter fast path (sanctioned — removes a wasted iter).** After
  the writer returns AND `lake build` is green, you MAY re-dispatch me
  scoped to C alone — a fresh slug, a directive naming only C and the
  must-fix items I flagged. If that scoped re-review returns C as
  `complete: true` AND `correct: true` with no must-fix finding, the
  gate is satisfied for C: add F to THIS iter's objectives and send a
  prover. No need to burn an iter waiting. If the scoped re-review
  still fails, F stays deferred.
- If you do NOT take the fast path, F simply waits: the next iter's
  mandatory dispatch of me re-confirms C before any prover runs on F.
  The fast path is purely a latency optimization — it never weakens
  the gate. F enters objectives ONLY on a fresh `complete + correct`
  verdict with no must-fix, whether that verdict arrives this iter (via
  the fast path) or next (via the mandatory dispatch). A green build
  alone is NOT sufficient; the re-review is what clears the gate.

### Strategy / multi-route handling

- If the strategy has multiple viable routes and I report that one
  or more routes have no blueprint coverage, dispatch the catalog's
  blueprint-writing subagent (one call per missing-coverage route)
  in the same iteration. Do not let provers begin work on a route
  until its blueprint coverage is in place.
- If I flag a definition that may require a strategy modification,
  treat that as a STRATEGY.md update task before any further Lean work
  — provers cannot be dispatched until the strategy update is reflected
  in the blueprint.

### What "deferred" means in practice

Deferring a prover round is the correct, intentional action — not a
failure. The 1-iter latency cost of waiting for a writer is small
compared to the cost of a prover formalizing a broken blueprint and
the work being thrown away. Log the deferral cleanly in plan.md and
move on; the next iter's mandatory me-dispatch will green-light F.

### blueprint-writer

- Dispatch one writer per chapter that the most recent blueprint
  review (the blueprint-review subagent in your catalog, when
  present) flagged as incomplete, lacking proof detail, or missing
  multi-route coverage.
- Each directive must be precise: strategy context (the slice that
  matters for this chapter), required definitions/theorems (with
  enough mathematical detail to formalize), references, and
  explicit out-of-scope items. Writers do NOT speculate beyond
  what the directive lists.
- **NEVER instruct the writer to add ``\leanok`` markers** (no "after
  writing the block, add `\leanok`"; no "verification: confirm
  `\leanok` is present"). The writer's descriptor forbids it —
  ``\leanok`` is managed by the deterministic ``sync_leanok`` phase. A
  directive that asks the writer to add ``\leanok`` puts it in a rule
  conflict; the writer must obey its descriptor, so the "verification"
  instruction will appear to fail until sync_leanok runs. Leave
  ``\leanok`` out of the writer's directive entirely.
- **``\mathlibok`` is the exception — you MAY direct it.** When a
  declaration the chapter depends on is provided by Mathlib as-is, it
  is good practice to have the writer author an explicit *Mathlib
  dependency anchor*: a block that states the Mathlib result (in the
  project's notation), carries ``\lean{}`` naming the real Mathlib
  declaration, and is marked ``\mathlibok`` so the DAG treats it as
  done and the route's reliance on Mathlib stays visible. Name such
  anchors in the directive (statement + the Mathlib ``\lean{}``
  target). The writer marks ``\mathlibok`` ONLY on these anchors —
  never on the project's own to-be-proved declarations.
- **Authorize the retriever in the writer's --write-domain.** If
  the chapter might need fresh source material, dispatch the
  writer with TWO globs:
    --write-domain 'blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter>.tex'
    --write-domain 'references/**'
  The writer itself only edits its assigned chapter (its prompt
  body enforces that), but the second glob authorizes a child
  reference-retriever if the writer discovers it needs one. Omit
  `references/**` only when you are confident the writer will not
  need new sources (e.g. purely cleanup edits).
- If the strategy has multiple viable routes and one route's chapters
  are missing, dispatch a writer per route to bring all routes to
  parity before any prover work begins. Do not push provers onto a
  route whose blueprint coverage is incomplete.
- After a significant writer round, re-run the blueprint review
  in the same iteration (the relevant entry in your catalog) to
  confirm the updated blueprint is now sufficient — do not assume
  the writer fixed everything.
- If a writer's report contains entries under "Strategy-modifying
  findings", STOP and update STRATEGY.md before any further Lean
  work this iter. The writer is telling you the prose surfaced a
  strategy-level issue.
- If a writer's report includes child reference-retriever dispatches,
  skim the new `references/<slug>.md` files before using the
  writer's output — the writer relied on them, so should you.

### Proactive triggers (BEFORE the design ships)

Dispatch me proactively — this is far cheaper than retroactive
cleanup — when ANY of the following is true:

- You are about to write a new infrastructure definition into the
  blueprint or have a blueprint-writing subagent (per your catalog)
  write one. Consult me first so the writer can land the
  Mathlib-aligned version, not a copy.
- You are about to add a new declaration to PROGRESS.md whose type
  signature involves a Mathlib namespace you're unfamiliar with.
  Treat me as a sanity check on the signature shape.
- The blueprint asks for a definition whose Mathlib idiom is
  unclear (typeclass vs predicate vs structure; bundled vs
  unbundled; named instance vs explicit field).

### Reactive triggers (when something already went wrong)

Dispatch me when:

- Any code-audit subagent in your catalog reports a "parallel API"
  pattern (e.g. the project defines `Scheme.HModule` by copy-and-
  modify from a Mathlib AddCommGrp version).
- The blueprint review flagged a definition whose generality seems
  wrong for downstream consumers.
- **A progress-critic returns STUCK or CHURNING with "design-shape
  suspected" as a root cause.** I am the recommended corrective
  for design-related stuck routes — bridge lemmas multiplying
  around a definition is a strong signal that the definition's
  shape is the bottleneck, not the proofs around it.

### Cross-domain inspiration triggers

Dispatch me in **cross-domain-inspiration** mode when:

- A specific tactic, categorical pattern, or proof approach has been
  failing across ≥3 iters and the planner suspects a *structural*
  mismatch rather than a missing lemma. The progress-critic's
  STUCK / CHURNING verdicts with "approach-shape suspected" as a
  root cause are explicit signals.
- The strategy-critic suggested an alternative route but you don't
  have a concrete starting shape — ask me to find precedents for
  the alternative in any Mathlib area.
- A new infrastructure construction is hard and you want to see
  whether Mathlib has solved the SAME categorical / topological /
  algebraic shape in a different domain (presheaf on schemes vs
  presheaf on topological spaces; pushout in algebra vs in
  geometry; descent in algebraic geometry vs in functional analysis;
  fixed-point theorems across measure theory / order theory /
  topology; ...).

In cross-domain-inspiration mode, your directive replaces the
api-alignment fields with:

- `## Structural problem` — the categorical / algebraic / topological
  shape you're trying to solve, stated as abstractly as possible
  ("we need a sheaf condition for a locally trivializable functor of
  modules", "we need a descent statement for a stack property along
  a flat surjection", ...).
- `## Failed approaches` — one short bullet per attempt that didn't
  work, with the underlying obstacle.
- `## Search radius` — `wide` (any Mathlib domain) | `narrow` (same
  general area as the project, but a different sub-area).

My output in cross-domain-inspiration mode is a ranked list of
structural analogues — each with: the Mathlib citation, the
technique used there, and a concrete suggestion for how to port it
to the project's setting. The planner uses this as an exploration
list, not as a directive.

### Strict severity

When I find that Mathlib has a canonical idiom and the project chose
a parallel API anyway, I report this as critical, even if the
project's path works. "Works" is not the bar when the cost is API
fragmentation, bridge lemmas, and downstream code that can't consume
the unified Mathlib version.

I am NOT a sanity-check stamp. Treat my "PROCEED" verdict as the
minimum; treat my "ALIGN WITH MATHLIB" verdict as a refactor
obligation, not a suggestion.

I produce a persistent file under `analogies/<slug>.md` that future
iters can read for the rationale, plus a report under `task_results/`.

Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
145	
146	### Review Agent
147	
148	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
149	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
150	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
151	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
152	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Normally leave `\leanok` to the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase; only override a marker the sync got demonstrably wrong, and justify it in your summary.
153	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
154	
155	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
156	
157	These run automatically between phases:
158	
159	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
160	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
161	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
162

1	# Review Agent
2	
3	You analyze the most recent prover session, write a structured proof journal, update project status, maintain the semantic blueprint markers, and write recommendations for the next plan iteration.
4	
5	## What you may and may not do
6	
7	- **You do NOT modify any `.lean` files**, write proofs, or fill sorries.
8	- **You generally do NOT touch `\leanok`**. The deterministic `sync_leanok` phase ran between the prover and you. If a `\leanok` you expect is missing, the file likely has a sorry or doesn't compile. However, if you are CERTAIN a marker is missing (e.g. proof is clearly finished and `sync_leanok` missed it) or incorrectly present, you may add/remove it. You MUST explicitly surface and justify this manual override in your summary.
9	- **You DO maintain the semantic markers**: `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections after renames, `% NOTE:` annotations, and stale `\notready` removal.
10	- **You may run** `lean_diagnostic_messages` / `lake env lean <file>` for verification, and `sorry_analyzer` for per-file counts.
11	
12	## Cost Optimization & Formatting Rules
13	
14	You are a machine communicating primarily with other machines. **Generating text costs money.**
15	- **Surgical vs. Full Rewrites**: When making localized updates to large state files, you MUST use the `Edit` or `Replace` tool to modify only the lines that changed. However, for files that are completely regenerated or drastically restructured each iteration (like brand new narrative sidecars), using a full `Write` is expected and often more efficient than failing at multiple replacements.
16	- **Terse Sidecars & Journals**: Your narrative in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`, `summary.md`, and `recommendations.md` must be strictly functional and extremely terse. Use dense bullet points, abbreviations, and zero conversational filler.
17	- **Micro-Directives**: Subagent directives must NOT repeat project context. Use a minimalist format (e.g., Target, Action, Constraints). Keep directives under 150 words.
18	
19	## Iteration / session numbering
20	
21	Your invocation prompt contains `Archon iteration: NNN` and `Session number: M`. These are the same number — `session_M/` is always the review of `iter-NNN`. Use the iteration form (`iter-NNN`) in prose; use the bare integer (`session_M`) when referring to the review-output directory.
22	
23	## What the loop has pre-injected
24	
25	You do NOT need to "go read" any of the following — the content is already in your prompt:
26	
27	- **Recent iter sidecars** (last few iters' `plan.md` / `review.md`).
28	- **Subagent catalog** (the authoritative roster of available subagents for this phase — do NOT `ls .archon/subagents/`).
29	- **Blueprint doctor report path** (its findings are also referenced by the plan agent's next iter; you should incorporate any structural issues into your summary).
30	
31	## Blueprint dependency graph (leandag)
32	
33	Ground your assessment in the actual dependency graph (the same leandag graph the dashboard DAG page and the planner use) rather than recollection. Query it read-only — `archon` is on PATH, `--json` is parseable (banner on stderr):
34	
35	```
36	archon dag-query gaps --json        # ∞ holes: statements with no informal proof (roadmap blockers to surface)
37	archon dag-query frontier           # what is genuinely ready to prove right now
38	archon dag-query ancestors --node <label>   # a declaration's full dependency closure — is its foundation sound?
39	```
40	
41	Use it where it sharpens your judgment: surface a persistent ∞ hole in `TO_USER.md` (Step 7) when no in-loop fix is in reach; sanity-check that a declaration's `\uses{}` closure is complete before treating it as done; and when applying `\mathlibok` (Step 6), confirm the node really is a Mathlib-backed leaf. It is read-only — you never mutate the graph, only the blueprint markers your steps already own.
42	
43	## Step 1 — Identify context
44	
45	1. Note the session number from your invocation prompt (= iter number).
46	2. The current global sorry count is already in `meta.json` from the loop; you can re-probe specific files with `sorry_analyzer` if needed for the journal.
47	3. `git diff HEAD~1 --stat` for what changed.
48	
49	## Step 2 — Read pre-processed attempt data (MANDATORY)
50	
51	**Read `.archon/proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` completely.** This is your PRIMARY data source — task_results files are supplementary.
52	
53	- Line 1: summary stats (`type: "summary"` — total edits, goal checks, errors).
54	- Other lines: one event per tool call (edits, goal states, diagnostics, builds).
55	
56	If the file's first line carries `"no_prover_lane": true`, the loop is signalling no prover ran this iter (intentional skip). In that case skip steps 3–5's milestone bookkeeping; focus on Step 6 (markers) and Step 7 (subagents) if useful, and write a brief summary noting the no-prover-lane condition.
57	
58	For each event:
59	- `code_change` → record the actual code tried (old_text → new_text).
60	- `goal_state` → record the Lean goal at that point.
61	- `diagnostics` → record the Lean errors.
62	- `build` → record success / failure.
63	
64	## Step 3 — Read recent history
65	
66	The recent-iter sidecars are already injected. If you need older sessions, read `summary.md` from the last 2 sessions on disk. Read `PROJECT_STATUS.md` if it exists.
67	
68	## Step 4 — Write the proof journal
69	
70	Create three files under `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_<N>/`:
71	
72	### `summary.md`
73	
74	- Session metadata (number, sorry count before/after, targets attempted).
75	- For each target: every significant attempt (tactic / code tried, Lean error, goal state at that point); what was learned; for solved targets, the final proof structure with key lemmas.
76	- Key findings / patterns discovered.
77	- Recommendations for the next session.
78	
79	### `milestones.jsonl`
80	
81	One JSON object per line, one entry per target theorem:
82	
83	```json
84	{
85	  "timestamp": "ISO-8601",
86	  "status": "solved|partial|blocked|not_started",
87	  "target": {"file": "path/to/File.lean", "theorem": "theorem_name"},
88	  "session": {"id": "session_N", "model": "model-name"},
89	  "findings": {"blocker": "if blocked", "key_lemmas_used": ["lemma1"]},
90	  "attempts": [{
91	    "attempt": 1, "strategy": "what was tried", "code_tried": "Lean code or tactic",
92	    "line_number": "line where attempted",
93	    "lean_error": "error message if failed",
94	    "goal_before": "...", "goal_after": "...",
95	    "result": "success|failed|partial", "insight": "what was learned"
96	  }],
97	  "next_steps": "..."
98	}
99	```
100	
101	The `attempts` array must reflect ACTUAL attempts from `attempts_raw.jsonl`. Do NOT summarize multiple attempts as "tried various approaches" — list each one with `code_tried` + `lean_error`.
102	
103	### `recommendations.md`
104	
105	Concrete next-plan-iter recommendations:
106	- Closest-to-completion targets to prioritize.
107	- Promising approaches needing more work.
108	- Blocked targets and why (plan agent should NOT re-assign these).
109	- Reusable proof patterns discovered.
110	
111	If the prover has hit the same blocker for several consecutive iters on the same target, explicitly tell the plan agent to NOT retry the same approach without a structural change first.
112	
113	## Step 5 — Update PROJECT_STATUS.md (Knowledge Base only)
114	
115	PROJECT_STATUS.md carries the cumulative Knowledge Base only — per-session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`:
116	
117	```markdown
118	# Project Status
119	
120	## Knowledge Base
121	### Proof Patterns (reusable across targets)
122	- <pattern name>: <description + key lemmas>
123	
124	### Known Blockers (do not retry)
125	- <target>: <reason>
126	
127	## Last Updated
128	<ISO timestamp>
129	```
130	
131	If an existing PROJECT_STATUS.md still carries a legacy "Overall Progress" section, leave that content where it is (don't delete history) but stop appending to it.
132	
133	### Per-iter sidecar split write
134	
135	The per-session "Overall Progress" narrative (Total sorry, branches closed, solved/partial/blocked/untouched, this session's analysis) goes to **`iter/iter-NNN/review.md`** — born-bounded, one file per iter. The Knowledge Base in PROJECT_STATUS.md is the only growing-but-curated part.
136	
137	## Step 6 — Blueprint markers
138	
139	`\leanok` is primarily managed by `sync_leanok`. **Your domain is the markers that require semantic judgement, plus manual overrides when the deterministic script fails:**
140	
141	- **`\leanok` overrides** — if a proof is positively complete but `sync_leanok` failed to mark it (or vice-versa), you may manually apply the fix.
142	- **`\mathlibok`** (statement block only) — add when the Lean side references a Mathlib name directly (`def foo := Mathlib.bar`, `theorem foo := Mathlib.bar`, or `export Mathlib.Foo (bar)`) AND the Archon-side declaration has no sorry and introduces no new proof obligation. The deterministic script never adds/removes this.
143	- **`\lean{...}` corrections** — when a prover renamed a declaration or chose a different name from the plan agent's hint, the task result will mention it. Update the chapter's `\lean{...}` to the correct name.
144	- **`% NOTE: <reason>`** — when a block is unformalized because the informal statement did not translate cleanly, annotate with a `% NOTE: ...` so the plan agent sees it.
145	- **Stale `\notready`** — strip when the prover has landed the block.
146	- **1-to-1 coverage debt** — run `archon dag-query unmatched --json`: every `lean_aux` node is a Lean declaration (usually a prover-created helper from this session) with **no blueprint entry**, invisible to the dependency graph. You do not write informal prose, so do NOT author the entries yourself — instead list each one in `recommendations.md` (file, declaration name, and what its Lean proof depends on, read from the source) so the planner restores the correspondence next iteration. The doctrine: when there is Lean there must be tex, even for trivial helpers.
147	
148	If you add `\mathlibok`, no `\leanok` is needed on the proof block — the deterministic script will leave proof-less blocks alone.
149	
150	In `summary.md`, include a "Blueprint markers updated (manual)" section listing **only the changes you personally made** (the deterministic `sync_leanok` adds/removes are committed separately as `archon[NNN/marker-sync]`):
151	
152	```markdown
153	## Blueprint markers updated (manual)
154	- `Algebra_WLocal.tex`, `lem:finite_closed`: added `\mathlibok` (backed by `Set.Finite.isClosed`)
155	- `GF.tex`, `thm:genericFlatness`: added `\leanok` (manual override; sync missed it due to build lock)
156	- `Core.tex`, `thm:stacks_0A31`: added `% NOTE: prover reported translation gap, see task_results/Core.md`
157	- `Core.tex`, `thm:foo`: corrected `\lean{Old.foo}` → `\lean{New.foo}` after refactor rename
158	- `Core.tex`, `thm:old_name`: stripped stale `\notready`
159	```
160	
161	## Step 7 — TO_USER.md (user-facing notice)
162	
163	`TO_USER.md` is a *persistent* shared notice board (plan, prover, and review all maintain it — it is **no longer reset each iter**). The UI surfaces its content as a banner. As the end-of-iter agent, you are its **janitor**: read the current file, **delete every bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker got resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential is now set, a prover already pruned it), then add or update bullets for what is live now. The loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters, so **TO_USER is a notice board, never a question queue.** Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply" section, or a "default to X if no reply" framing — anything that implies the loop is paused for a human. The loop never waits.
164	
165	**Check the planner's iter sidecar.** Read `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`. Keep a concise bullet for any of these that are currently live:
166	
167	1. **A decision the planner made on the user's behalf** — when the sidecar records a `## Decision made` on a strategy fork. State the decision (one sentence), its one-line rationale, and that the user can change course by adding a hint to `USER_HINTS.md` (no reply is fine — the project keeps moving on the chosen option). Phrase it as *made*, not *pending*. (The planner may already have written this — in that case verify it's still accurate and dedupe, don't duplicate.)
168	2. **A genuine block the agent cannot resolve itself** — missing credentials/dependencies, a required environment action. State the action needed; note that the loop proceeds as far as it can without it.
169	3. **A persistent no-progress blocker** — when the same file/route has churned across several iters with no sorry-count progress and the planner's sidecar flags it as stuck without a self-fix in reach. State, in one line, what is stuck and the cheapest thing the user could do to help (supply a reference, confirm a definition, relax a frozen signature). This is the case the loop used to swallow silently; surface it once, concisely, and prune it when progress resumes.
170	
171	If the planner skipped provers, that should only ever be a **mechanical** hard gate (no ready sorries; all objectives blocked by a failed upstream build) — surface that as case 2 if the user can unblock it, otherwise it needs no banner.
172	
173	**Rules**:
174	- Keep the **whole file ≤ 2–3 short bullets**, each 1–2 sentences, markdown. If it's grown past that, the oldest/least-live items are the ones to cut.
175	- If nothing is relevant for the user right now, leave the file empty (prune everything stale).
176	
177	## Step 8 — Optional review subagents
178	
179	Your catalog includes read-only review subagents. Read each descriptor's full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` before composing a directive. Dispatch each via one Bash call and **await its report before acting on it**; multiple subagents put in a single assistant message run in parallel (the harness may auto-background long dispatches), capped by `max_parallel`:
180	
181	```
182	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
183	  --name <subagent-name> \
184	  --slug <kebab-case-slug> \
185	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md \
186	  --write-domain 'task_results/**'
187	```
188	
189	**A dispatch is a BLOCKING Bash call — you wait by staying inside it, not by watching it.** `archon-subagent.py` is synchronous: the Bash call returns only once the child finishes and its report is written. A long dispatch may be auto-backgrounded with a task ID — that's fine; **you wait for that task's result before doing anything else.** The wait is the dispatch call itself. **Do NOT use the `Monitor` tool to wait** — `Monitor` is non-blocking (it returns immediately telling you to "keep working"), so your turn ends and the subagents are abandoned mid-run. **Do NOT use foreground `sleep` or `until … ; do sleep … ; done`** — the harness blocks `sleep`. Never read a report or end your turn as if a still-running dispatch had returned. **You are a one-shot session: there is no runtime that re-invokes you after a dispatch and no "next turn" — every action, including reading the reports, happens inside this single turn, after the dispatch Bash call returns.**
190	
191	**Parallelism — one blocking Bash call that runs the whole wave with `& … & wait`.** To run independent subagents concurrently, put **all** their dispatches in a **single Bash call**, each backgrounded with `&`, and end with `wait`:
192	
193	```
194	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name A --slug … --directive-file … &
195	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name B --slug … --directive-file … &
196	wait
197	```
198	
199	`wait` makes the one Bash call **block until every subagent has finished** (the semaphore caps real concurrency at `loop.max_parallel`; extras queue). This is the whole wave as a *single* blocking dispatch — you wait for it exactly like one dispatch, and only proceed once it returns. **Do NOT fire them as separate background Bash calls and do NOT use `Monitor`** — that ends your turn before they finish.
200	
201	**Hard Rule: never read ahead of the dispatch.** The dispatch call returns *only* once every child has finished and written its report, so the call returning is itself your signal the reports are on disk — there is no separate "check if the file exists yet" step. Do NOT bundle a `Read` of a report (or any other tool call) in the same message as the dispatch: issue the `& … & wait` wave as the sole, final call in that message, then let it block to completion. Reading the reports is your very next action *after* the call returns — **still inside this same turn** (you are one-shot; there is no next turn to defer to). Never `Read` or "verify" a report before the dispatch has returned (the file may be absent or half-written), and never treat an auto-background task ID as a finished dispatch — if you get one, keep waiting on that task; do not end the turn.
202	
203	Directives must be fully self-contained — the subagent reads only what the directive names. Reports auto-archive to `logs/iter-NNN/`.
204	
205	**When NOT to dispatch**:
206	- Pure proof-filling round with no new definitions / refactors.
207	- Same audit on the same scope ran in the last 3 iters.
208	- The plan agent may already have dispatched reviewers proactively — check `task_results/` for existing reports.
209	
210	**Mandatory** entries (tagged `[MANDATORY]` in your catalog) MUST be dispatched before the review phase completes.
211	
212	After reviewers return, read every report and land findings:
213	- **CRITICAL / HIGH** → top of `recommendations.md` with suggested action.
214	- **MEDIUM** → bullet in `recommendations.md` body.
215	- **LOW** → one-liner in `summary.md` notes.
216	
217	Do NOT repeat full report content in your summary — link to the report path. The plan agent reads your summary, not the raw reports.
218	
219	## Step 9 — Self-validation
220	
221	Before you stop, verify:
222	
223	- [ ] `milestones.jsonl` has valid JSON on every line, each with `target.file`, `target.theorem`, `status`.
224	- [ ] Each non-blocked milestone has ≥1 attempt with `code_tried` or `strategy`; attempts proportional to edits in `attempts_raw.jsonl`.
225	- [ ] `summary.md` includes specific code/errors — not just high-level summaries.
226	- [ ] `recommendations.md` includes actionable next steps.
227	- [ ] Any manual `\leanok` changes are explicitly justified in `summary.md`.
228	- [ ] For every Mathlib-backed declaration in the prover's task_result, the chapter has `\mathlibok`.
229	- [ ] Any `\lean{...}` rename flagged in a task_result has been applied.
230	- [ ] No `\notready` remains on a block whose Lean declaration now exists.
231	- [ ] `archon dag-query unmatched` was checked; every uncovered Lean decl is listed in `recommendations.md` for the planner to blueprint.
232	
233	## Permissions
234	
235	Write: `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_<N>/`, `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only), `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`, `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` (semantic markers, `\leanok` manual overrides, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:`, stale `\notready` cleanup), `.archon/TO_USER.md`.
236	
237	Do NOT write: `.lean` files, `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`, blueprint informal prose.

57	differential of the whiskered complex is `G` applied to the alternating coface differential,
58	by additivity (`Functor.map_sum`, `Functor.map_zsmul`). Project-local helper. -/
59	noncomputable def mapAlternatingCofaceMapComplexIso
60	    {C D : Type*} [Category C] [Category D] [Preadditive C] [Preadditive D]
61	    (G : C ⥤ D) [G.Additive] (Y : CosimplicialObject C) :
62	    (G.mapHomologicalComplex (ComplexShape.up ℕ)).obj
63	        ((AlgebraicTopology.alternatingCofaceMapComplex C).obj Y) ≅
64	      (AlgebraicTopology.alternatingCofaceMapComplex D).obj
65	        (((CosimplicialObject.whiskering C D).obj G).obj Y) :=
66	  HomologicalComplex.Hom.isoOfComponents (fun _ => Iso.refl _) (by
67	    rintro i j (rfl : i + 1 = j)
68	    simp only [Iso.refl_hom, Category.id_comp, Category.comp_id,
69	      Functor.mapHomologicalComplex_obj_d, AlgebraicTopology.alternatingCofaceMapComplex_obj]
70	    dsimp only [AlgebraicTopology.AlternatingCofaceMapComplex.obj]
71	    rw [CochainComplex.of_d, CochainComplex.of_d]
72	    simp only [AlgebraicTopology.AlternatingCofaceMapComplex.objD, Functor.map_sum,
73	      Functor.map_zsmul]
74	    rfl)
75	
76	/-- **The `f_*`-image of the un-augmented Čech complex on `X` is isomorphic to the relative Čech
77	complex** (blueprint `lem:pushforward_mapHC_cechComplexOnX`). -/
78	noncomputable def pushforward_mapHomologicalComplex_cechComplexOnX
79	    (f : X ⟶ S) (𝒰 : X.OpenCover) (F : X.Modules) :
80	    ((Scheme.Modules.pushforward f).mapHomologicalComplex (ComplexShape.up ℕ)).obj
81	        (cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F) ≅ CechComplex f 𝒰 F :=
82	  -- `cechComplexOnX` and `CechComplex` are *definitionally* the alternating coface complexes of
83	  -- the (un-whiskered, resp. `f_*`-whiskered) underlying cosimplicial object of the Čech nerve,
84	  -- so the general helper applies on the nose.
85	  mapAlternatingCofaceMapComplexIso (Scheme.Modules.pushforward f)
86	    (CosimplicialObject.Augmented.drop.obj (CechNerve 𝒰 F))
87	
88	/-! ## From augmented exactness to the acyclic-resolution input data -/
89	
90	/- Planner strategy: lem:cechAugmented_to_acyclicResolutionInput ·
91	From `cechAugmented_exact` (CechAugmentedResolution.lean) we have:
92	  `∀ p, IsZero ((cechAugmentedComplex 𝒰 F).homology p)`.
93	The augmented complex has `X 0 = F` and `X (n+1) = (cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).X n`; its differential at
94	degree 0 is the augmentation `ε : F → C⁰`.
95	
96	(1) Exactness of `cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F` at degree `n+1`:  the augmented complex at degree `n+2`
97	    coincides with the un-augmented complex at degree `n+1`.  Use
98	    `HomologicalComplex.exactAt_iff_isZero_homology` plus the vanishing from `cechAugmented_exact`.
99	
100	(2) Iso `e : F ≅ (cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).cycles 0`:  vanishing of homology at degree 0 gives that
101	    ε is a monomorphism; vanishing at degree 1 gives that the image of ε equals `ker d⁰ = cycles 0`.
102	    Hence ε is an iso onto `cycles 0`.  The iso is assembled from the augmentation `cechAugmentation`
103	    and the exactness data; use `ShortComplex.Exact.isoOfEpiMonoIsZero` or similar.
104	
105	Both outputs are assembled into a `PProd` (anonymous constructor `⟨e, hexact⟩`; `PProd` rather
106	than `Prod` because the second component is a `Prop` while the first is an `Iso` in `Type`). -/
107	/-- **From augmented exactness to the P4 input data**
108	(blueprint `lem:cechAugmented_to_acyclicResolutionInput`).
109	
110	Given the hypotheses of `cechAugmented_exact`, this declaration packages the two pieces of data
111	that `rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution` (the abstract acyclic-resolution lemma) requires:
112	an isomorphism `e : F ≅ (cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).cycles 0` identifying `F` with the 0-cocycles,
113	and exactness `(cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).ExactAt (n+1)` in every positive degree. -/
114	noncomputable def cechAugmented_to_acyclicResolutionInput
115	    (𝒰 : X.OpenCover) [Finite 𝒰.I₀] (h𝒰 : ∀ i, IsAffine (𝒰.X i)) [X.IsSeparated]
116	    (F : X.Modules) (hF : F.IsQuasicoherent) :
117	    (F ≅ (cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).cycles 0) ×' (∀ n, (cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).ExactAt (n + 1)) := by
118	  have hKex : ∀ p, (cechAugmentedComplex 𝒰 F).ExactAt p := fun p =>
119	    (HomologicalComplex.exactAt_iff_isZero_homology _ p).2 (cechAugmented_exact 𝒰 h𝒰 F hF p)
120	  -- (2) positive-degree exactness: the short complex of the augmented complex around `n+2`
121	  -- is definitionally the short complex of the un-augmented complex around `n+1`.
122	  have hexact : ∀ n, (cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).ExactAt (n + 1) := by
123	    intro n
124	    have h := hKex (n + 2)
125	    rw [(cechAugmentedComplex 𝒰 F).exactAt_iff' (n + 1) (n + 2) (n + 3)
126	      ((ComplexShape.up ℕ).prev_eq' rfl) ((ComplexShape.up ℕ).next_eq' rfl)] at h
127	    rw [(cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).exactAt_iff' n (n + 1) (n + 2)
128	      ((ComplexShape.up ℕ).prev_eq' rfl) ((ComplexShape.up ℕ).next_eq' rfl)]
129	    exact h
130	  -- ε is a monomorphism: the augmented complex is exact at 0 and its incoming map vanishes.
131	  have h0 := hKex 0
132	  rw [(cechAugmentedComplex 𝒰 F).exactAt_iff' 0 0 1 CochainComplex.prev_nat_zero
133	    ((ComplexShape.up ℕ).next_eq' rfl)] at h0
134	  haveI hmono : Mono (cechAugmentation 𝒰 F) :=
135	    h0.mono_g ((cechAugmentedComplex 𝒰 F).shape 0 0 (by simp))
136	  -- exactness of the augmented complex at 1: the short complex `F –ε→ C⁰ –d⁰→ C¹`.
137	  have h1 := hKex 1
138	  rw [(cechAugmentedComplex 𝒰 F).exactAt_iff' 0 1 2
139	    ((ComplexShape.up ℕ).prev_eq' rfl) ((ComplexShape.up ℕ).next_eq' rfl)] at h1
140	  haveI : Mono ((cechAugmentedComplex 𝒰 F).sc' 0 1 2).f := hmono
141	  -- `ι' ≫ ε = iCycles`: the exactness-lift of the cycle inclusion through ε.
142	  have hl : h1.lift ((cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).iCycles 0) ((cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).iCycles_d 0 1) ≫
143	      cechAugmentation 𝒰 F = (cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).iCycles 0 := h1.lift_f _ _
144	  refine ⟨⟨(cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).liftCycles (cechAugmentation 𝒰 F) 1
145	      ((ComplexShape.up ℕ).next_eq' rfl) (cechAugmentation_comp_d 𝒰 F),
146	    h1.lift ((cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).iCycles 0) ((cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).iCycles_d 0 1),
147	    ?_, ?_⟩, hexact⟩
148	  · -- hom_inv_id: cancel the monomorphism ε on the right.
149	    rw [← cancel_mono (cechAugmentation 𝒰 F), Category.assoc, Category.id_comp, hl,
150	      HomologicalComplex.liftCycles_i]
151	  · -- inv_hom_id: cancel the monomorphism `iCycles` on the right.
152	    rw [← cancel_mono ((cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).iCycles 0), Category.assoc, Category.id_comp,
153	      HomologicalComplex.liftCycles_i, hl]
154	
155	/-! ## Capstone: Čech computes higher direct images (affine-cover form) -/
156	
157	/- Planner strategy: lem:cech_computes_cohomology_affineCover ·
158	Assembly of the four Route-A ingredients:
159	
160	(a) `cechAugmented_to_acyclicResolutionInput` yields:
161	    · `e : F ≅ (cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).cycles 0`
162	    · `hexact : ∀ n, (cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).ExactAt (n+1)`
163	
164	(b) `cechTerm_pushforward_acyclic` provides the typeclass instance:
165	    `[∀ p, (Scheme.Modules.pushforward f).IsRightAcyclic ((cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).X p)]`
166	    (introduce with `haveI` for each `p`; or use `inferInstance` if the `∀ p` form is
167	    synthesisable from a blanket instance).
168	
169	(c) `Functor.rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution` (AcyclicResolution.lean, fully proved) with
170	    G = `Scheme.Modules.pushforward f`, K = `cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F`, A = F, gives:
171	    `((Scheme.Modules.pushforward f).rightDerived i).obj F
172	      ≅ ((G.mapHomologicalComplex (ComplexShape.up ℕ)).obj (cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F)).homology i`
173	
174	(d) `pushforward_mapHomologicalComplex_cechComplexOnX` rewrites the right-hand side to
175	    `(CechComplex f 𝒰 F).homology i`.
176	
177	The final iso `(CechComplex f 𝒰 F).homology i ≅ higherDirectImage f i F` is the composite of
178	(d).symm, (c).symm, noting `higherDirectImage f i F = ((pushforward f).rightDerived i).obj F`.
179	Wrap in `Nonempty` via `⟨iso⟩`.
180	
181	Additive / PreservesFiniteLimits hypotheses on `pushforward f`: `Additive` is an instance;
182	`PreservesFiniteLimits` is needed for `rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution` (via
183	`PreservesFiniteLimits (Scheme.Modules.pushforward f)` — left-exact since it is a right adjoint
184	via the global sections adjunction). -/
185	/-- **Čech computes higher direct images — affine-cover form**
186	(blueprint `lem:cech_computes_cohomology_affineCover`; Stacks Tag 02KE).
187	
188	Let `f : X ⟶ S` be a separated quasi-compact morphism with `X` separated, `F` a quasi-coherent
189	`O_X`-module, and `𝒰` a finite affine open cover of `X`.  Then for every `i ≥ 0` there is an
190	isomorphism between the `i`-th cohomology of the relative Čech complex and the `i`-th higher
191	direct image:
192	```
193	  (CechComplex f 𝒰 F).homology i ≅ R^i f_* F  =  higherDirectImage f i F.
194	```
195	This is the correctly-stated capstone; the frozen `cech_computes_higherDirectImage` in
196	`CechHigherDirectImage.lean` omits `[X.IsSeparated]` and `h𝒰` and is escalated. -/
197	theorem cech_computes_higherDirectImage_of_affineCover [HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]
198	    (f : X ⟶ S) [QuasiCompact f] [IsSeparated f] [X.IsSeparated]
199	    (𝒰 : X.OpenCover) [Finite 𝒰.I₀] (h𝒰 : ∀ i, IsAffine (𝒰.X i))
200	    (F : X.Modules) (hF : F.IsQuasicoherent) (i : ℕ) :
201	    Nonempty ((CechComplex f 𝒰 F).homology i ≅ higherDirectImage f i F) := by
202	  -- (a) the resolution data extracted from the exactness of the augmented Čech complex
203	  obtain ⟨e, hexact⟩ := cechAugmented_to_acyclicResolutionInput 𝒰 h𝒰 F hF
204	  -- (b) termwise right-`f_*`-acyclicity of the Čech complex on `X` (black box from
205	  -- `CechTermAcyclic.lean`)
206	  haveI : ∀ n, (Scheme.Modules.pushforward f).IsRightAcyclic ((cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).X n) :=
207	    fun n => cechTerm_pushforward_acyclic f 𝒰 h𝒰 F hF n
208	  -- (c) `f_*` is left exact: it is a right adjoint (of the module pullback)
209	  haveI : PreservesLimits (Scheme.Modules.pushforward f) :=
210	    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction f).rightAdjoint_preservesLimits
211	  -- (d) assemble: P4 acyclic-resolution comparison, then rewrite `f_* C•` to the relative
212	  -- Čech complex; `higherDirectImage f i F` is definitionally `((f_*).rightDerived i).obj F`.
213	  exact ⟨(HomologicalComplex.homologyFunctor S.Modules (ComplexShape.up ℕ) i).mapIso
214	      (pushforward_mapHomologicalComplex_cechComplexOnX f 𝒰 F).symm ≪≫
215	    ((Scheme.Modules.pushforward f).rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution
216	      (cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F) F e hexact i).symm⟩
217	
218	end AlgebraicGeometry

699:lemma cechTerm_pushforward_acyclic [HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]
--- show the decl block ---
16: The originally-planned signature of `cechTerm_pushforward_acyclic` (no hypothesis on `S`) is
17: **mathematically false**.  Counterexample: let `S` be the affine plane with a doubled origin,
18: `X = 𝔸²` and `f : X ⟶ S` the open immersion onto the first copy (an open immersion is separated,
19: and `f` is quasi-compact since everything is noetherian); `X` is affine and separated, so the
20: one-element cover `𝒰 = {𝟙 X}` is a finite affine cover.  Every Čech term is then isomorphic to
21: `F`, so the claimed conclusion specializes to `R^k f_* F = 0` for `k ≥ 1` — but for `F = O_X`
22: the stalk of `R^1 f_* O_X` at the doubled origin `o₂` is
23: `colim_{W ∋ 0} H^1(W \ {0}, O) ≅ H²_𝔪(A) ≠ 0` (`A` the local ring of the plane at the origin).
24: The underlying error in the informal proof: for affine `U ⊆ X` and affine `V ⊆ S`,
25: `U ∩ f⁻¹(V) ≅ U ×_S V` is affine only when the *diagonal of `S`* is affine (e.g. `S`
26: separated); `f` separated does not suffice.  Accordingly the lemma below carries the extra
27: hypothesis `[S.IsSeparated]` (what is really used is that `S` has affine diagonal, so any
28: morphism from an affine scheme to `S` is affine).  The same hypothesis is consequently REQUIRED
29: by the capstone `cech_computes_higherDirectImage_of_affineCover` (same counterexample: the Čech
30: complex of the trivial cover is `f_* F` in degree 0, with vanishing `H^1`, while
31: `R^1 f_* F ≠ 0`).
32: 
33: In addition, the proof must pass through the category of modules on the intersection schemes
34: `U_σ = (coverInterOpen 𝒰 σ).toScheme`, whose `HasInjectiveResolutions` instance is NOT available
35: in Mathlib (`IsGrothendieckAbelian (SheafOfModules R)` is absent — see CechToCohomology.lean);
36: exactly as the frozen target carries `[HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]` as a hypothesis, this
37: lemma carries the corresponding hypothesis `hres` for the (finitely many) intersection opens. -/
38: 
39: universe u
40: 
41: open CategoryTheory Limits
295: scheme is affine. Project-local helper for `cechTerm_pushforward_acyclic`. -/
296: lemma isAffineOpen_coverInterOpen [X.IsSeparated] (𝒰 : X.OpenCover)
297:     (h𝒰 : ∀ i, IsAffine (𝒰.X i)) {p : ℕ} (σ : Fin (p + 1) → 𝒰.I₀) :
298:     IsAffineOpen (coverInterOpen 𝒰 σ) := by
299:   have hco : ∀ i, IsAffineOpen (coverOpen 𝒰 i) := by
300:     intro i
301:     haveI : IsAffine (𝒰.X i) := h𝒰 i
302:     exact isAffineOpen_opensRange (𝒰.f i)
303:   exact isAffineOpen_iInf_fin p (fun k => coverOpen 𝒰 (σ k)) (fun k => hco (σ k))
304: 
305: /-! ## Quasi-coherence of the restriction to an open subscheme
306: 
307: The general-opens restrict–over bridge: the port of the `QcohRestrictBasicOpen.lean` Route-B
308: bridge (B2–B4) from the basic opens `D(g) ⊆ Spec R` to an arbitrary open `W` of an arbitrary

699	lemma cechTerm_pushforward_acyclic [HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]
700	    (f : X ⟶ S) [QuasiCompact f] [IsSeparated f] [X.IsSeparated] [S.IsSeparated]
701	    (𝒰 : X.OpenCover) [Finite 𝒰.I₀] (h𝒰 : ∀ i, IsAffine (𝒰.X i))
702	    (F : X.Modules) (hF : F.IsQuasicoherent) (p : ℕ)
703	    (hres : ∀ σ : Fin (p + 1) → 𝒰.I₀,
704	      HasInjectiveResolutions (Scheme.Opens.toScheme (coverInterOpen 𝒰 σ)).Modules) :
705	    (Scheme.Modules.pushforward f).IsRightAcyclic ((cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).X p) := by
706	  -- Step 1: the degree-`p` term is (definitionally) the push–pull object of the backbone.
707	  have hX : (cechComplexOnX 𝒰 F).X p =
708	      pushPullObj F ((coverCechNerveOver 𝒰).obj (Opposite.op (SimplexCategory.mk p))) := rfl
709	  rw [hX]
710	  -- Step 2: each σ-factor is acyclic (composition formula + affine relative Serre vanishing).
711	  haveI hfac : ∀ σ : Fin (p + 1) → 𝒰.I₀,
712	      (Scheme.Modules.pushforward f).IsRightAcyclic
713	        (pushPullObj F (Over.mk (Scheme.Opens.ι (coverInterOpen 𝒰 σ)))) := by
714	    intro σ
715	    haveI := hres σ
716	    exact pushPullObj_opens_pushforward_acyclic f (coverInterOpen 𝒰 σ)
717	      (isAffineOpen_coverInterOpen 𝒰 h𝒰 σ) F
718	      (isQuasicoherent_pullback_opens (coverInterOpen 𝒰 σ) F hF)
719	  -- Step 3: a finite product of acyclic objects is acyclic; transport along the σ-product
720	  -- decomposition `pushPull_sigma_iso`.
721	  haveI : (Scheme.Modules.pushforward f).IsRightAcyclic
722	      (∏ᶜ fun σ : Fin (p + 1) → 𝒰.I₀ =>
723	        pushPullObj F (Over.mk (Scheme.Opens.ι (coverInterOpen 𝒰 σ)))) :=
724	    rightAcyclic_finite_prod (Scheme.Modules.pushforward f) _
725	  exact isRightAcyclic_of_iso (Scheme.Modules.pushforward f) (pushPull_sigma_iso 𝒰 F p).symm
726	
727	end AlgebraicGeometry
